title: Sparse undercut-cover features with monotone-constrained XGBoost
summary: Game-theoretic F1 work reports that accounting for competition materially changes optimal pit timing, but the worst local runs suggest broad opponent feature dumps are too noisy.
rationale: Game-theoretic F1 work reports that accounting for competition materially changes optimal pit timing, but the worst local runs suggest broad opponent feature dumps are too noisy. A narrower version may work: build a few high-signal rival-response scores, then regularize them with monotone constraints so the tree cannot learn implausible reversals.
implementation_hint: Create only a small set of causal rival features: nearest car ahead/behind tyre-age gap, same-compound pace gap, whether a nearby rival pitted in the previous lap, and a cover-threat score for top positions. Train a separate XGBoost with monotone constraints on physically directional variables like tyre age, degradation stress, and undercut threat, then test it as an ensemble member rather than replacing the baseline.
expected_effect: Adds a genuinely different model family bias and may improve hard strategic cases where pit timing depends on one or two nearby rivals, not the full field average.
risk: Wrong monotonic assumptions or inaccurate rival proxies can flatten useful interactions and reduce raw AUC.
